rubric.task_summary: Implement efficient prime check for n up to 10^7 in Python with correct edge case handling.
failure_modes: Incorrect primality logic for small numbers
Time limit exceeded for large inputs
Missing edge case handling for n <= 1

assumptions: Input n is always an integer.
The maximum value of n is 10,000,000, which fits within standard integer types and allows for O(sqrt(n)) complexity to pass within 1 second.
known_risks: If n is extremely large (much larger than 10^7), the O(sqrt(n)) approach might become slow, but this is within the specified constraint.

assumptions: The input n will always be an integer.
The maximum value for n is 10,000,000, which fits within standard integer types and allows for O(sqrt(n)) complexity to pass within 1 second.
known_risks: If n is very large (close to 10^7), the loop runs up to sqrt(n) which is ~3162 iterations, which is well within time limits.
Incorrect handling of negative numbers if not checked at the start, though the logic handles n <= 1 explicitly.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The maximum value of n is 10,000,000, which fits within standard integer types.
The function will be called with valid integer inputs.
known_risks: If n is very large (much larger than 10^7), the O(sqrt(n)) complexity might become slow, but this is within the specified constraint.
For extremely large numbers, more advanced primality tests (like Miller-Rabin) might be needed, but for 10^7, trial division is sufficient and correct.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is for a single call to is_prime(10,000,000) or a reasonable number of calls involving such values.
known_risks: Trial division is O(sqrt(n)). While extremely fast for n=10^7 (sqrt(n) approx 3162), if the task implied checking all primes up to 10^7 repeatedly, a Sieve of Eratosthenes would be more appropriate.
Python's integer division and modulo operations are efficient, but for much larger numbers (e.g., > 10^15), Miller-Rabin would be required.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is sufficient for a single primality test of a number up to 10^7 using O(sqrt(n)) complexity.
The function will be called for individual numbers rather than requiring a sieve for multiple queries (though O(sqrt(n)) is fast enough for single calls).
known_risks: If the task implied checking *all* numbers up to 10,000,000 (a sieve), a single function call approach might be slow for repeated calls, but for a single check, O(sqrt(n)) is optimal.
Floating point precision issues if using math.sqrt(), though i * i <= n avoids this.

assumptions: The input n is an integer as per the type hint.
The performance requirement is measured against a single call for the maximum value or a reasonable number of calls; the 6k+/-1 optimization is sufficient for n=10^7 in Python.
known_risks: While O(sqrt(n)) is efficient for a single check, if the task implied checking all numbers up to 10^7 (sieve), this function would be too slow. However, the signature asks for a single integer check.
Python's interpreter overhead might be a concern for very large numbers of calls, but for n=10,000,000, sqrt(n) is ~3162, which is well within the 1s limit.
